### Curiosity
-   The question isn’t how to introduce wonder — it’s how not to destroy it
-   Andre Geim (Nobel Prize
column2: graphene): insisted his lab “mess around” on Friday afternoons; graphene was discovered that way

column1: -   Geim is the only person to win both the Ig Nobel and Nobel Prize (levitating frogs → graphene)
-   Iterative development as the path to good ideas
-   Cox does 40–50 small shows (300-seat theatres) before finalising a tour — the show “finds its own form”
-   Direct parallel to product: don’t expect perfection at the gate; the idea is just the beginning
### AI in Education & First Principles Thinking
-   AI is an excellent “non-judgmental tutor” — useful for deepening understanding without ego or impatience
-   The goal is to deepen your own understanding
-   Risk: students (and product people) using AI to answer known questions without internalising anything
-   First principles thinking remains valuable — but it’s not universally superior
-   Particle physics: first principles works because the system is simple enough
-   Biology/complex systems: you have to look at the whole; reduction doesn’t always apply
-   Neither approach is more fundamental — depends on the problem
### Science Communication & Public Obligation
-   Scientists have an obligation to explain their work — taxpayer money is the simple reason; civilisational stakes are the deeper one
-   The modes of thought from Kepler/Galileo/Newton underpin the modern world; if we stop explaining them
-   On engaging with hostile or misinformed audiences: don’t dismiss — redirect
-   Carl Sagan’s taxi driver (asking about Atlantis
column2: UFOs): the correct response isn’t mockery
column3: it’s recognising genuine curiosity that’s been failed by institutions

column1: 74a677f1-294e-417a-a4eb-3014a5a87857;kinga@productkind.com;Matt LeMay;productkind;2026-06-19T14:42:21.893Z;"### Core Thesis: AI Creates the Illusion of Alignment
-   AI is better at repeating what we say than asking what we meant
-   “Dig in” anecdote: Claude rewrote a customer-facing Google Doc unprompted — had interpreted “dig in” as full ownership
-   We often don’t know what we mean until we execute against it *with other people*
-   AI homogenizes language → creates *feeling* of alignment without actual alignment
-   Recent study: positive AI effects on collaboration may reflect only linguistic alignment
-   New pope’s encyclical framed it as “the new Tower of Babel” — prioritizes conformity over communion
-   Project Voltron case study: whole team used same language
column3: but had completely different ideas of scope

-   Goal: surface real misalignments

column1: - Opus 4.6 described as “an absolute game changer” for agent quality
- Evals now managed via LangSmith; agent routing centralized for billing tracking and tracing
- On MVP thinking in the AI era: competing against established tools with tech-savvy users raises the quality bar — rushing a low-quality release would have been counterproductive
- Hardest part wasn’t building — it was the moving target of how product leaders’ understanding of AI kept shifting month to month
- Prioritization method: 2–3 specific problems to solve per week; used alpha/beta users’ deadlines to impose discipline

b4e104a2-27a3-485b-aebf-60b7aeba91e4;kinga@productkind.com;Rodolfo Cristóvão & Mike Yeardly;productkind;2026-06-19T11:31:13.163Z;"### Core Thesis
- Scaling isn’t a skills problem — it’s an identity problem
- PMs must let their current identity die to be reborn at each growth stage
- What works at 100 people gets you fired at 500; same again from 500 to 900
- Feedzai context: grew from \~100 to 900 people; processes 120B events/year
column3: protects \~1B customers from fraud

column1: ### The 5 Mutations
1. **Snowflake → Platform**
- Early stage: said yes to every customer’s custom schema and UI — felt heroic
column3: logos multiplying

column1: - Reality: fragmentation; product held together by 3–4 engineers who couldn’t take holidays
- Shift: built a cross-channel platform because criminals don’t respect org charts — fraud spans cards
column3: credit
column4: cash-out simultaneously

column1: 2. **Platform → Categoric Architect**
- Platform success attracted praise — but compliments = “you’re becoming my professional services org”
- Moved from winning individual customer battles to winning the war (market-level thinking)
- Shift: from generalists and doers → architects
column3: guiders

column1: 3. **Order-taker → Strategic Filter (saying no)**
- Needed courage
column3: to say no

column1: - Key: never say no in isolation — say no *with evidence*
column3: and cross-customer insight

column1: - Customer quote: *“Sometimes we hang ourselves to the extent of the rope that you provide us”*
- Detached from the emotional reward of individual “yes” moments
4. **UX-centric → Moat-centric (GenAI)**
- GenAI can now replicate months of UI/UX polish in under a day
- If your product is just UI
column2: someone in a garage with two new grads can copy it

column1: - Feedzai’s moat: 120B events → proprietary models that detect new fraud patterns faster than anyone else
- Question every PM should ask: *what is your moat?*
5. **Direct → Partner-led**
- Scaling to $200–500M ARR requires partners — can’t get there with your own company alone
- PMs move further from direct customer relationships and personal gratification
- Shift: from delivery to enablement; from getting the love to multiplying it through partners
- New output: API docs

column1: ### One Product
column2: Five Pricing Models — The Competitive Landscape Has Exploded

column1: - A single enterprise client (€400K/year) can have 5 stakeholders with 5 different pricing preferences:
1. IT Head (Ian) — cost per employee; compares on price
2. Head of Delivery (Dana) — searches/matches; alternatives include internally built AI agents
3. Operations (Oliver) — successful matches only; doesn’t care about users or search volume
4. Chief Revenue Officer (Rachel) — eliminating bench time; alternatives are consultants or internal AI builds
5. Head of HR (Helen) — pay only for successful projects (% of contract value); if vendor charges 2% of revenue
column2: internal build becomes easy to justify

column1: - The pricing model you choose changes who your competitor is — and vice versa
- Competition is no longer just other SaaS tools: includes internal builds

column1: - Hands-on leadership ≠ doing your engineer’s job
- It means staying close enough to the truth that you don’t need someone to translate it for you
- AI removes the excuse for not looking — it compresses discovery
### The Zalando Problem
- Remit: inspiration and discovery for 60M+ customers — moving people from deeply held preferences to trying something new
- Initial metrics failed: WAU/MAU looked healthy but couldn’t distinguish “inspired” from “frustrated”
- Built an **engagement value system**: mapped actions (follows
column3: add-to-cart) between impressions and GMV

column1: - Showed correlation between engagement score and downstream revenue
- Unlocked investment from market leaders
- Root cause turned out to be the author experience
- 400+ content authors (1
column2: 000+ including influencers) creating content for 60M customers

column1: - Trapped across 10+ overlapping tools — CMS
column3: tagging
column4: configuration — mostly not talking to each other

column1: - No unified roadmap; some tools abandoned but still in use
- Oktoberfest campaign running indefinitely because someone marked it “evergreen” — illustrative of the data rot
### The Fix
- Surbhi used Claude Code to read every repository across 10+ tools — not to build
- Checked API call history to identify which features were actually used vs. built-but-ignored
- One week → complete map of the chaos
- Quantified the cost: millions of Euros/year lost to internal friction
- Took findings to leadership framed as opportunity
- “We’re spending X maintaining systems that do identical jobs — sunset 3
column3: free up capacity”

column1: - “Campaign creation takes weeks — fix the foundation
column2: it takes minutes. Here’s the revenue unlock”

column1: - Made the counterintuitive call: **stopped all new customer-facing features** until the author foundation was fixed
- Rationale: personalization algorithms are only as good as the content supply feeding them
- Fixing author workflows unblocked the content engine → better downstream customer experience
- Result: 7
column3: creation time reduced from weeks to minutes; personalization algorithm finally well-fed

column1: ### Two KPIs Nobody Expected
1. **Adoption by other teams** — not whether your own team uses a feature
column2: but whether other teams build on top of it

column1: - Signal of leverage: did you solve a shared problem or just your own?
2. **Feature sunset rate** — how many features has your team retired this quarter?
- Duplication is inevitable at scale; unbounded duplication is a tax on speed
column3: and cognitive load

column1: - Sunsetting = discipline + courage
### Three Takeaways
1. Hands-on leadership is a strategic choice — staying close to the work keeps decisions grounded in truth
2. Use AI yourself before delegating insight generation — tools exist to compress discovery without a consultant or a quarter-long process
3. Fix the foundation before the customer experience — the most impactful product decision is often not building something new";;"Speaker A: Here.

column1: 1207bd33-5004-4eb5-bb81-09b30563fcad;kinga@productkind.com;April Dunford;productkind;2026-06-16T15:03:51.427Z;"### Positioning Fundamentals (The Framework)
-   Positioning = defining how your product is the best in the world at delivering value a well-defined set of customers cares a lot about
-   Five components:
1.  Competitive alternatives (if you didn’t exist
2.  Distinct capabilities (what do you have that competitors don’t?)
3.  Differentiated value (what does that capability *do* for the customer’s business?)
4.  Best-fit customers (who cares most about that value?)
5.  Market category (what context best frames the product?)
-   Aim for max 3 value themes — not one per feature
### Four AI-Era Positioning Pitfalls
-   **AI doesn’t change the fundamentals** — but creates ~100 new ways to mess it up
-   Pitfall 1: No point of view on the future
-   Customers (especially non-tech buyers) are scared and confused — ~50% of deals end in no decision because everything looks the same
-   Your POV should explain *why your roadmap looks the way it does*

-   Examples: Anthropic (AI takes over dev tasks) vs. OpenAI (intelligence as utility) vs. Replit (agents spin up tools on demand) vs. Microsoft (context engineering is the differentiator — and they own the context) vs. Salesforce (APIs/back-end is where the value lives) vs. ServiceNow (governance and workflows are the whole game)
-   POV must be rooted in your actual differentiated value — not invented from scratch
-   Pitfall 2: Positioning against the wrong alternatives
-   Product teams think about horizon competitors; marketing fears whoever spends most on ads; sales only tracks short-list competitors (never counts status quo)
-   CEOs either have a preserved-in-amber view of the market from years ago
column2: or pitch investor-vision so often it bleeds into their competitive view

column1: -   Rule: position against who you *actually* compete with right now — not who you expect to compete with in 18 months
-   Example: company in IT management space — CPO wanted to position against vibe-coding tools (2 months old
column2: never appeared in a deal); sales had never seen them on a short list

column1: -   Pitfall 3: Selling too far into the future
-   If you pitch the 2-year vision
column3: come back in 2 years” — kills urgency

column1: -   Sell the value customers get *right now* for *right now* dollars
-   For forward-looking AI products: draw a maturity model — meet customers where they are today (e.g. step 1: map processes; step 2: automate low-risk tasks; step 3: full agent orchestration)
-   Pitfall 4: Not systematizing customer understanding
-   Most companies — even large
column2: sophisticated ones — don’t do this consistently

column1: -   Four mechanisms that work:
1.  Customer advisory board — ask them directly when internal debates arise
2.  Executive customer sponsorship — each exec owns 4 accounts
3.  Win/loss analysis — wins reveal what’s actually working and who you’re really beating
4.  Regular positioning check-ins — quarterly or bi-annual speed round with sales: any new competitors on short lists? Has anyone caught up on capabilities?
### Resources
-   *Obviously Awesome* (2019) — original positioning book
-   *Sales Pitch* — new book

17161540-ae88-49ef-8ea7-c012e6883eec;kinga@productkind.com;Rich Mironov;productkind;2026-06-19T16:06:09.276Z;"### The Money Stories Framework
-   Go-to-market execs (sales
column3: finance) actively don’t care about product processes — they hear “tech debt
column4: ” “backlog
column5: ” “discovery” as excuses for not delivering

column1: -   When product teams use process language
column2: the GTM side hears: “reasons you can’t have what you want”

column1: -   Solution: speak in money stories
column2: not user stories — every sentence needs a currency symbol

column1: -   Money story formula: ≤3 numbers
column3: SWAG estimates (factor of 4–5 accuracy is enough)

column1: -   4-digit opportunities: deprioritise without discussion
-   6- and 7-digit opportunities: the only ones worth the room’s attention
-   Example (upsell): 60
### The Three Stories Product Leaders Must Always Have Ready
**Story 1 — Earning our keep (ROI story)**
-   R&D is a profit center
column2: not a cost center — without product
column3: there’s nothing to sell

column1: -   Average maker team costs ~€1M/year; most companies need at least 6x return to cover the rest of the org
-   Frame: “For every €1 you spend on us
column2: we bring in €8 — give us more money to leverage that ratio”

column1: -   Real question being answered: do we trust you to spend our money well?
**Story 2 — The roadmap story**
-   Roadmap amnesia: execs forget the roadmap the moment any big customer asks for something not on it
-   Standard roadmaps invite the wrong question (“when will we have it?”) instead of “why does this matter?”
-   Fix: add bold monetary estimates to each roadmap item
-   Execs immediately ignore the boxes and focus on the numbers
-   Payoff: when a customer calls asking for a diversion
column2: the answer becomes “the roadmap is worth €2–5M/year; this deal is worth €80K — it’s a bad trade”

column1: -   Shifts argument from tech debt/architecture to business value
**Story 3 — Discovery and GTM convert code to revenue**
-   AI/speed thesis: if engineering can ship 100 features/day
column2: 99 will never reach a user without discovery and go-to-market

column1: -   Bottleneck has moved from development to: user attention
column3: positioning
column4: pricing
column5: channels

column1: -   Code ≠ product; code becomes product only when paired with messaging
column3: packaging

column1: -   Prediction: failure rates on new features will spike as teams confuse shipping speed with product success
-   Coming revaluation: product management becomes more important
column3: as the build bottleneck disappears

column1: -   Whoever figures out what to build and how to bring it to market extracts the value
### Takeaways
-   Three things the rest of the org forgets every 15 minutes:
1.  R&D creates the things that generate revenue — sales gets the commission
2.  Items are on the roadmap because they’re expected to deliver monetary value — not because of a prioritisation framework
3.  Discovery and GTM strategy are what turn code into revenue
-   Rich Mironov’s book covers the money stories framework in full (copies available at the event)

column1: 6656108a-a1b3-4834-ba82-63a08cc8c1a2;kinga@productkind.com;Tania Nemes;productkind;2026-06-19T10:34:36.753Z;"### Context: Uncertainty as Strategy
Tania Nemes
column2: Director of Product at Dynatrace (formerly Adidas running & training apps)
column3: spoke about leading through radical uncertainty after Adidas laid off 33% of the sports apps team in Feb 2023 — same goals
column4: no playbook
column5: no timeline clarity.

column1: -   Core reframe: stop waiting for clarity; “learn your way forward”
-   Framing shift: “win or learn” (not “win or lose”) — removes fear of failure
column2: makes every setback a data point

column1: -   Key tension she named: publicly preaching learning while privately afraid of every failure — that contradiction drove the three shifts below
### Three Shifts
**1\. Proximity — get closer to reality**
-   Became obsessed with understanding user churn at Adidas running
-   Found: users doing only 1 activity/month → 60% wouldn’t open app next month; 80% of those never re-engaged
-   Identified “dormant users” as the key opportunity space
-   Brought the metric to the team with no answers — invited them to destroy it
-   Team flagged it as too lagging; dozens of questions surfaced
-   Iterated through Power BI dashboards
column3: spreadsheets until landing on a concrete KPI

column1: -   Final target: get users from 1 activity → 3 activities/week
column2: with specific duration and distance thresholds

column1: -   Question to ask yourself: *“Am I close enough to reality to know my next step?”*
**2\. Hypothesis-driven product work**
-   Every piece of product work = a question to be answered
column2: not an answer to be executed

column1: -   No room for “failure” — only: did we learn fast enough?
-   Structured around three pillars: Believe
column3: Educate

column1: -   Example: “weekly running streak → award Adidas Club points” hypothesis
-   Team challenged: do users care about points? Is gamification the right lever?
-   Changed all check-ins: “what did you learn since last time?” replaced “what did you deliver?”
-   Key question to ask: *“What data point tells you this won’t work?”* — most people can answer why something will work
**3\. People — anchor in themselves
-   Stopped removing uncertainty for the team; instead helped them root in their own identity and working style
-   Used the **Strength Continuum** framework
-   Every strength
column3: becomes a weakness (e.g. curiosity → innovative vs. lost in noise
column4: delayed decisions)

column1: -   Ran workshops helping PMs identify signals that they’re overplaying vs. optimising a strength
-   At Dynatrace now: building strong leadership teams per domain so teams anchor in each other
-   Question to ask: *“Am I giving my people clarity
column2: or helping them learn without it?”*

column1: -   Clarity creates dependency; capability is what you need in uncertain times
### Results & Resources
-   86% employee retention rate — 20 months after a 33% layoff
-   60% above company goals one year after the layoffs
-   80% increase in monthly active users (target goal)
-   23% above sales target from Adidas sports apps
Tania offered to share:
-   Zip folder of workshops
column3: and methodologies for teams and stakeholders

column1: -   Talks on the people/strengths side
-   Available via email and LinkedIn (QR code shared on stage)

column1: 694cfed4-4cb9-4dcb-8b40-7088550c0089;kinga@productkind.com;Krasi Bozhinkova;productkind;2026-06-19T08:44:53.678Z;"### The Speed Trap
-   Krasi Bozhinkova (fractional CPO) talk: “How AI makes speed a commodity — and what that means for product strategy”
-   Core thesis: speed is an amplifier
-   Good signal + speed = great; chaos + speed = efficient failure
-   Market data undermining the “build more” instinct:
-   Software growth rates declining consistently for 5–6 years
-   CAC rising from <$2 to ~$3 per $1 of new revenue
-   Payback period: was 12-month gold standard
-   Old bottleneck: engineering capacity; new bottleneck: judgment
-   The harder question is no longer “can we build it?” but “what do we need to build?”
### Cautionary Tales vs. Heroes
Cautionary:
-   Humane — shipped fast without reliability; 30% of users returned devices; closed in 2025
-   ChatGPT Store — resembled a flea market; no pricing differentiation
column3: no monetization path for developers

column1: -   Duolingo — “AI-first” strategy read as cost-cutting; stock dropped 20%+ in a single day; users canceled before touching the product
-   Snapchat My AI — had the capability
column2: not the user trust or permission; users couldn’t remove it

column1: Heroes:
-   Framework — starts with a trust promise (modular computing
column3: not a feature list

column1: -   reMarkable — protected the core aha moment (“think clearly here”) by saying no to AI features
column3: apps

column1: -   Miro — recognized new behaviors worth scaling; rebuilt workflow integration rather than just adding AI on top
-   Lovable — turned a major security incident (public source code exposure) into a product signal; fixed UX
column3: and code to close the perception gap

column1: ### Frameworks & Q&A Takeaways
Three questions to ask before building:
1.  What do we need to protect?
2.  What do we need to prove?
3.  How do we learn before we scale?
On when to go fast vs. slow (Krasi):
-   Go fast when confident value creation is understood
-   Any doubt → pause
column2: return to critical user need and value model

column1: On wasted development:
-   Cost isn’t lower — it’s hidden and distributed
-   Misallocation shows up as brand damage
column3: and cognitive overload for customers

column1: On what to protect vs. retire:
-   Identify the most impactful component of your value (the aha moment)
-   Protect that; resist pressure on everything else — saying no is a strategic act
On “hacking”:
-   Prototyping is one form of learning
-   Test trust and business viability with proof of commits

column1: -   Figma example: designer Daniela given 8-week estimate to ship a feature

-   Presented to engineers → estimate dropped from 8 weeks to 2

-   Got pushback from tech initially; routed through Head of Platform instead
-   Product managers successfully raising PRs — until a ransomware false alarm prompted a pause and reintroduction of specialist oversight
### The Friction Framework
-   Three types of friction to distinguish:
1.  Remove — friction between person and outcome (tool access
2.  Protect — friction that enforces quality gates
column3: judgment (e.g.
column4: the ransomware moment)

column1: 3.  Change — friction that can be automated or handed to agents over time
-   Risk of AI tooling: “first version becomes the version” — divergent thinking gets skipped
-   Dylan Field (Figma CEO) has flagged this as the fidelity trap
-   Stakeholders shown prototypes assume it’s shippable; sets false expectations
-   “Slop” risk: spinning things up quickly gets stakeholders excited before proper judgment is applied
### Specialist Skills in an AI World
-   Skills/prompts in AI tools can lower the floor — but high-judgment domains (accessibility

column1: 944925d3-4624-4cb3-9056-6c8166ed0cff;kinga@productkind.com;Dave Ross;productkind;2026-06-19T11:50:12.499Z;"### The Core Shift: Scarcity → Abundance
-   Old mindset: protect precious engineering resources (the “Dr. No” PM)
-   New mindset: agents accelerate delivery → rethink the roadmap from scratch
-   Roadmaps were built with a bias toward what was practical in a given timeframe
-   AI doesn’t make bottlenecks disappear — it moves them
-   Risk: functions accelerate individually without shared context → speed becomes chaos
-   MCP helps with tooling fragmentation but doesn’t fix ways of working
-   The real shift: from efficiency to **taste and judgment**
### Reshaping the Discovery → Definition → Delivery Loop
-   Agents are collapsing definition; delivery is already accelerating → product people must shift deep left into continuous discovery
-   Discovery can no longer be a start/stop process at project kickoff
-   Prototyping is having its “big breakthrough” — second only to agentic coding
-   Old: designers go away
column2: come back weeks later with 1–2 iterations

Central argument from Gary Crawford & Kevin Stewart (Willingdale Advisory): every AI future splits on one question — does AI serve the human
column2: or does it serve the system?

column1: Three characters illustrate this across two versions each (utopian vs. dystopian):
-   Priya (CEO of TripAdvisor competitor): utopian = AI helps her build a new vision
column2: shift from selling a search box to delivering outcomes; dystopian = board pressure leads her to offload accountability onto AI
column3: accept bad customer outcomes as “acceptable risk”

column1: -   Marcus (product owner / super contributor): utopian = AI as subservient tool
column3: tests before production; dystopian = AI pushes straight to production
column4: flatters him into accepting it
column5: he can’t discern the risk

column1: -   Maya (end consumer planning a Lisbon trip): utopian = AI surfaces options tailored to her preferences
column2: she retains agency and final decision; dystopian = company retains the interface
column3: AI optimises for partner packages
column4: Maya gets a “good enough” outcome but loses agency and doesn’t know what she’s missing

column1: Key insight: most dystopias are built from utopian promises — the differences between the two futures are subtle
### Three Business Model Shifts
1.  **What your organisation sells** is changing
-   SaaS market values in steady decline over the past year
-   “Software is eating the world” is reversing — digitalising product is now commoditised by AI subscriptions
-   New opportunity: digitalising *services*
column3: not tools

column1: 2.  **How you organise to create value** is changing
-   Individual “super contributors” can now achieve what large teams previously required
-   Lean AI-native companies as evidence:
-   Midjourney: $200M ARR in 2 years
-   Cursor: similar trajectory
-   ElevenLabs: $100M in 2 years
-   Lovable: $10M ARR in 10 months
-   Implication: sprawling teams with complex communication overhead are no longer the default path
3.  **How your organisation is accessed** is changing
-   Businesses no longer reliably own the customer interface (app
-   Customers — or agents acting on their behalf — access capabilities through chat/AI interfaces that aggregate across providers
-   The challenge shifts from “taking product to customer” to “exposing your capabilities and data so AI can consume them on the customer’s behalf”
### AI Capability Signals Worth Knowing
-   ChatGPT: 800M weekly users
column3: 5th most visited website globally

column1: -   Reached 100M monthly active users faster than any prior platform
-   AI IQ benchmarks (rough mapping): GPT-4 ~80 IQ → median models ~90 → O3 ~160 → “Mythos-class” models now sanctioned by US government
-   Capability doubling roughly every 5 months (per leading experts)
-   Text-to-video (Sora 2) now indistinguishable from real footage for non-specialists
-   Presenters’ framing: LLMs are statistical prediction machines — next character → next word → next sentence → emergent human-like voice; all language interactions are now in scope
> Willingdale Advisory works with leaders on AI strategy

column1: c44d4978-1e97-45c9-9bf1-aff0947571ef;kinga@productkind.com;Martin Eriksson;productkind;2026-06-19T16:34:26.940Z;"### AI as a Growth Lever
-   Predictions of job destruction are almost always wrong on timing and direction
-   Geoff Hinton predicted radiologists would be replaced within 5–10 years; 10 years later there are 16% more radiologists
-   AI made imaging 50% faster and 10x more accurate → cost fell → demand exploded → largest radiologist shortage in history
-   Jevons Paradox: when something gets cheaper
column2: we don’t do less of it — we do vastly more

column1: -   GenAI reached the same user penetration as the internet in 2 years instead of 20
-   Cost of any given level of AI falls ~10x every 12 months (Sam Altman)
-   The “build vs. buy” shift is real but smaller than the tech bubble suggests — 84% of people have never used GenAI; only 0.04% have tried coding tools
-   IKEA case: AI reduced customer service load → instead of firing staff
column2: retrained them as interior designers → new business line generated $1.4B in year one

column1: -   Intercom/Fin: rebuilt from scratch as an AI-first chatbot
column2: flipped pricing from per-seat to $0.99 per resolution → Fin hit $100M ARR in under a year → acquired by Salesforce for ~$4B

column1: -   Kaive (Sweden): pivoted from AI asset management to brand-safe generative asset creation (e.g. Polestar generates car imagery without flying vehicles to shoot locations)
### The Decision Stack Framework
-   Five questions every product org must answer:
1.  Where are we going? → **Vision**
2.  How do we get there? → **Strategy**
3.  What matters right now? → **Objectives**
4.  What actions do we take? → **Opportunities**
5.  How do we choose between actions? → **Principles**
-   Too many companies apply AI only at the Opportunities layer — sprinkling features without strategic intent
-   The moats have changed: scale economies
column3: and switching costs are increasingly moot

column1: -   New moats: distribution
column3: proprietary data
column4: domain expertise
column5: deep customer understanding
column6: trust and brand

column1: -   None of these are technology investments — all compound over time
-   The old 5-year plan refreshed annually is dead; scenario planning must become a continuous discipline
### Q&A Takeaways
-   On selling product discovery internally: don’t use the word “discovery” — frame it as due diligence or risk reduction; executives understand risk language
-   On strategy when it only lives in a deck: don’t wait for top-down clarity — build a strawman strategy with your team and push it upward; ICs can and should own strategy
-   On platform team ROI: tie value to either revenue (aggregate demand model across dependent product teams) or cost savings (e.g. inference cost reduction)
-   On vision and strategy in an AI world: the fundamentals haven’t changed — vision is still about the customer problem
column2: strategy is still about market/customer/product choices; the *answers* have changed

Leadership coach specializing in neurodiverse leaders; ADHD + dyslexic; ~7
column3: hundreds of leaders

column1: -   “Masking tax” — the cognitive cost of appearing neurotypical
-   Neurodiverse brains spend 5–6x more tokens on ordinary social/meeting interactions
-   ~20% of general population is neurodiverse; ~45–50% in tech (per Microsoft
-   “Signal drift” — when communication quality lags behind thinking quality
-   Leaders get pigeonholed as “gets the job done” rather than “strategic” — not due to thinking
column2: but to how they land messages

column1: -   Classic symptom: told “you’re not executive enough” or “not strategic enough” despite doing strategic work all day
### Three Common Mistakes
1.  **Not translating** — speaking your vocabulary
-   Example: “product discovery” fell flat with a board; reframed as “due diligence before committing budget” → immediate buy-in
-   Same activity
column2: same outcome — just framed in investor language

column1: 2.  **Not defining the signal** — walking into meetings unprepared
-   Especially costly for neurodiverse brains; even 5 minutes of prep makes a significant difference
3.  **Not leaving the room** — letting the room control the conversation instead of you
-   Status updates are the classic trap: reactive
column3: no influence

column1: ### Signal Prep Framework (3 Steps)
1.  What do I need from the room?
-   Reframe every meeting as an influence opportunity
2.  What’s my recommendation — short
column3: tight?

column1: -   Lead with the recommendation; let them pull the detail through questions
-   Giving too much detail upfront invites off-topic questions and loses control
3.  What’s the retell?
-   Craft a snappy phrase people will repeat when you’re not in the room
-   Example: “minutes
column2: not months” — ended up appearing in documents the client never attended

column1: - Talk structured around two contrasting case studies: launching AI at scale vs. from scratch
### Case Study 1: AI Feature at Scale — Google Search Personalization
- Google Search has shifted from search engine → answer engine over the past 3 years
- AI Overview (AI snippet at top of results) launched \~1 year ago
- AI Mode (dedicated tab for in-depth AI-first conversations) is the current focus
- Pain point identified: users must constantly re-explain personal context and preferences with each search
- Hero use cases chosen: local dining and shopping
- Key challenge: standard metrics didn’t capture the value of personalization
- “Helpfulness” is partly subjective — had to redesign the metric to capture that
- Launched via Search Labs (opt-in) to constrain to millions of users
- Evaluation challenge: privacy restrictions mean human raters can’t access personal activity data
- Solution: synthetic users + automated raters to process private data safely at scale
- Personalized automated evaluation was still an active AI research problem — solved in-house via DeepMind
- Takeaways:
1. Match AI advantages to real pain points
2. Prove the feature beats the existing experience on its own terms
3. Spot technical blockers early — get AI research aligned from day one
### Case Study 2: AI-Native Product from Scratch — Evans Foundation Digital Citizenry
- Problem: political decision-makers rely on polls (weeks-long
column3: expensive) — no fast

column2: simulating public opinion in real time

column2: 000 real surveys mapped to 100+ human values

column1: ac04b8fd-e773-4706-860f-401ba2dbb16f;kinga@productkind.com;John Moriarty;productkind;2026-06-16T13:03:31.318Z;"### Fin’s AI Productivity Results
- Goal set 12 months ago: double R&D productivity using AI within a year
- Progress flat for first few months; new models arrived December 2024 and changed everything
- Outcome: tripled productivity (measured by PRs per person per month across R&D — design
column3: engineering)

column1: - 94% of PRs now authored by Claude Code
- 19% of PRs auto-approved with no human reviewer in the loop
- 2x product changes shipped
- 9 major (Tier 1) product launches in 2 months
### How Roles Are Shifting
- “Agents own the middle” — when 94% of code is AI-authored
column2: human focus moves to the edges

column1: - PM shifts further upstream: highest-leverage customer conversations and strategy
- Design splits: further upstream (contributing to roadmap) and further downstream (contributing to front end)
- Engineering splits: larger end-to-end projects + building technical foundations the whole system runs on
- Iteration cycle compresses — what took weeks now takes days; same team runs multiple streams in parallel
- New bottlenecks: problem framing
column3: quality judgment
column4: product coherence across a surface everyone is contributing to simultaneously

column1: ### Three Focus Areas for Design
1. **Upstream direction** — deciding what’s worth building; increasing ownership of roadmap
- Example: a designer built a working prototype of “Procedures” (merges probabilistic + deterministic workflows in a text interface) connected to real customer data
column2: iterated with customers in real time — no PRD process

column1: 2. **Infrastructure** — design systems become the quality rails that agents and teams build upon
- Fin built “Surge” (design system) and “Surge Intelligence” — codifies taste and patterns in a format Claude can consume
- Components
column3: and design/engineering decisions encoded so AI can leverage them

column1: 3. **Quality** — getting to 80% is now instant; the risk is quality degrading silently as volume accelerates
- Coherence doesn’t happen automatically; must be built in
- Craft shifts from controlling every detail to setting the conditions that enable good work across the org
### Products Themselves Are Changing
- UI is thinning — agents are displacing entire categories of screens
- Fin’s “Operator” agent: replaces Topics Explorer UI — instead of navigating dashboards
column2: you tell it what you need and approve the output

column2: update the help centre” → it does it

- Categories disappearing: navigation screens

column1: - The question every product team will face: *what’s left of your product when AI handles the jobs it was designed to do?*
